content.text: Containerised agentic services separate runtime configuration from application code so that the same Docker image can run identically in development, staging, and production — differing only in the values injected at startup through environment variables, mounted secrets, and external configuration stores. Getting this layer right is one of the most operationally impactful decisions in an agent deployment: a misconfigured model name, a missing API key, or an inadvertently enabled destructive tool has caused real production incidents.

Environment variables are process-level key-value pairs that the operating system makes visible to every child process. In a container context, the container runtime (Docker, containerd) populates the container's environment from several sources: the
instructions baked into the Dockerfile, values passed with
flag, and — in orchestrators such as Kubernetes or Amazon ECS — environment entries projected from ConfigMaps, Secrets, or Parameter Store references.
Application code reads those values at runtime through the standard process environment — for example,

(JS/TS) build a typed, validated settings layer on top, adding default values, type coercion, and mandatory-field enforcement so that a missing critical value raises a clear error at import time rather than causing a confusing runtime failure.

For agentic systems the configuration surface is larger than for a typical API server. In addition to infrastructure endpoints, an agent container typically needs settings that govern LLM model selection, per-tool enable/disable flags, safety limits (maximum reasoning steps, allowed-tools list), and observability endpoints — all of which may differ between deployment environments or between the API process and background worker processes in the same service.

The core principle at stake is the Twelve-Factor App's factor III: "store config in the environment". When a service embeds configuration directly in source code or in the Docker image, any change — even swapping the model name from
— requires rebuilding and re-pushing the image, which breaks CD pipelines, complicates rollbacks, and forces the CI system to touch production secrets.
For agentic pipelines the stakes are even higher. An agent that calls external APIs, modifies databases, or sends emails is acting in the world. Deploying with the wrong
flag, or pointing at a production database because

defaulted to prod instead of staging, has caused data-loss incidents. Fail-fast validation — verifying every required setting before the service begins accepting requests — is therefore more important for agents than for read-only microservices.
Environment-driven config also enables important operational workflows: canary deployments can run a new model on a small fraction of traffic by differing only in

; zero-downtime secret rotation becomes possible because the new key is injected by the secrets manager without touching source code or image layers; and per-environment feature flags let teams test experimental tools on staging before enabling them in production.
— the same Docker image artifact is deployed to staging and then to production without rebuild; only the environment changes.
— a new API key is injected by the secrets manager and the container restarted; no code change, no re-deploy of the image.
— pydantic-settings raises at import if a required field is absent or if a string can't be coerced to the declared type, preventing the service from starting in a broken state.
on staging keeps costs and blast radius low while the flag is true on prod.
— every config change is a delta in the orchestrator's task definition or ConfigMap, recorded in git history and deployment logs with actor and timestamp.
— developers commit code, operators inject secrets, without either party needing access to the other's domain.
— the industry-standard pattern for cloud-native services, well-understood by tooling, platforms, and team members alike.
— a service with 30+ variables needs discipline: naming conventions, a committed
, and documentation. Without this, operators set the wrong variable name and the bug is silent.
— environment variables are always strings. Complex nested config (a list of allowed tool names, a tree of rate-limit rules) is awkward to encode; a mounted YAML/JSON file is cleaner for those cases.
Secrets in plaintext in the process environment
— any code running in the same process or any collaborating sidecar can read every variable. Prefer short-lived tokens and Kubernetes projected volumes for tightly-scoped secrets.
— changing an environment variable requires a container restart. Feature flags that must flip without a restart need a dedicated flag service (LaunchDarkly, Flipt) or a config watcher pattern.
— if two containers that should share config each receive their variables from different sources, they can drift out of sync silently. Centralise shared config in a single ConfigMap or SSM namespace.

When a containerised process starts it receives its environment from several layers. The precedence order matters: a later layer overrides an earlier one. The typical hierarchy, from lowest to highest priority, is:
Hard-coded defaults inside the application code (e.g.
file read by pydantic-settings or python-dotenv (local development only — never deployed to production).
instructions — sets values baked into the image layer (use only for non-secret, rarely-changing defaults like
Orchestrator environment entries: Kubernetes ConfigMap env projection, ECS task definition environment array — non-secret config per environment.
Orchestrator secret references: Kubernetes Secret envFrom, ECS Secrets field pointing to AWS Secrets Manager or Parameter Store — sensitive values never appear in the task definition JSON.
pydantic-settings resolves these layers in a single pass at import time: it reads the process environment first, then optionally merges a
file, then fills gaps with declared defaults. Any field with no resolved value and no default triggers a
All environment variables arrive as strings. pydantic-settings applies Python type coercion before constructing the settings model. This means
is truthy in Python. That bug has enabled destructive agent tools in production.
pydantic-settings uses a dedicated bool-parsing rule: the strings

A well-designed settings class logs its effective configuration at INFO level immediately after validation, with all secret fields replaced by a redacted marker. This gives operators a clear audit trail — "what settings was this container actually running with?" — without leaking keys into log aggregators.

The diagram below shows the full lifecycle of configuration into a running agentic service, from the upstream sources (secrets manager, ConfigMap, feature-flag service) through the container's process environment, through the pydantic-settings validation layer, and finally into the agent runtime that drives LLM calls, tool execution, and safety controls.

attrs.alt: Architecture diagram: config sources → container ENV → pydantic-settings → agent runtime

content.text: Three classes of config flow in from the left: secrets (API keys, database credentials) arrive from the Secrets Manager via the orchestrator's secret reference mechanism, never touching source code; non-secret config (model names, rate limits, URLs) comes from a ConfigMap or equivalent; feature flags arrive either as env vars (static per deployment) or from a flag service (dynamic). All values converge in the container's process environment and are validated by pydantic-settings at startup. The validated settings object is then consumed by the agent runtime's three main sub-systems: the LLM client configuration, the tool-execution layer, and the safety governor.
— API keys, database passwords, webhook signing secrets. These must never appear in source code or image layers.
— model name, log level, feature enable flags, infrastructure endpoint URLs.
Operational knobs that differ between environments
Settings that must change without rebuilding the image
— A/B test model variants, cost-control toggles.
Prefer mounted config files (ConfigMap volume mounts or S3-fetched YAML) for:
— a YAML file of 20 allowed tool names with per-tool rate limits is readable; encoding it in a single env var is not.
— system prompts and few-shot examples that are updated independently of the agent code.
— content-safety rule sets, tool permission matrices.

Use a dedicated feature-flag service (LaunchDarkly, Flipt, Unleash) when flags need to flip in real time without a container restart, when you need targeting rules (e.g. flag on for 5% of requests), or when you need rollback in under a second. Environment-variable flags restart the container; dynamic flag services do not.
Step 1 — Define a typed settings class

Start with a pydantic-settings model that declares every setting the service needs, along with its type, an optional default, and a description. Group agent-specific settings into nested sub-models for clarity.

# settings.py
from __future__ import annotations
from typing import Literal, List
from pydantic import field_validator, model_validator
from pydantic_settings import BaseSettings, SettingsConfigDict


class LLMSettings(BaseSettings):
    model_config = SettingsConfigDict(env_prefix="LLM_")
    api_key: str              # LLM_API_KEY — no default, required
    model: str = "gpt-4o"    # LLM_MODEL
    base_url: str = "https://api.openai.com/v1"
    timeout_seconds: float = 60.0
    max_tokens: int = 4096


class AgentSettings(BaseSettings):
    model_config = SettingsConfigDict(env_prefix="AGENT_")
    max_steps: int = 25
    allowed_tools: List[str] = ["web_search", "calculator", "code_exec"]
    require_human_approval: bool = False
    sandbox_enabled: bool = True

    @field_validator("max_steps")
    @classmethod
    def steps_in_range(cls, v: int) -> int:
        if not (1 <= v <= 200):
            raise ValueError(f"max_steps must be 1–200, got {v}")
        return v


class Settings(BaseSettings):
    model_config = SettingsConfigDict(
        env_file=".env",
        env_file_encoding="utf-8",
        extra="ignore",
    )
    # Infrastructure
    redis_url: str = "redis://localhost:6379/0"
    database_url: str
    log_level: Literal["DEBUG", "INFO", "WARNING", "ERROR"] = "INFO"
    environment: Literal["development", "staging", "production"] = "production"

    # Nested models
    llm: LLMSettings = LLMSettings()
    agent: AgentSettings = AgentSettings()

    def log_safe(self) -> dict:
        """Return settings dict with secrets redacted for startup logging."""
        d = self.model_dump()
        d["llm"]["api_key"] = "***REDACTED***"
        if "password" in d.get("database_url", ""):
            d["database_url"] = d["database_url"].split("@")[-1]  # hide creds
        return d


# Module-level singleton — raises ValidationError at import if config invalid
settings = Settings()
Step 2 — Log effective config at startup

# main.py
import logging
from settings import settings

logging.basicConfig(level=settings.log_level)
log = logging.getLogger(__name__)

log.info("Starting agent service with config: %s", settings.log_safe())
# Proceeding after this line means config is valid

# .env  (local development — gitignored)
LLM_API_KEY=sk-dev-...
LLM_MODEL=gpt-4o-mini
DATABASE_URL=postgresql://user:pass@localhost:5432/agentdb
AGENT_MAX_STEPS=10
AGENT_SANDBOX_ENABLED=true
LOG_LEVEL=DEBUG
ENVIRONMENT=development

# kubernetes/configmap.yaml  (non-secret, checked into infra repo)
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: agent-config
data:
  LLM_MODEL: "gpt-4o"
  LLM_TIMEOUT_SECONDS: "60"
  AGENT_MAX_STEPS: "25"
  AGENT_SANDBOX_ENABLED: "true"
  LOG_LEVEL: "INFO"
  ENVIRONMENT: "production"
---
# kubernetes/secret-ref.yaml  (secret fetched from AWS Secrets Manager at pod start)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: agent-secrets
type: Opaque
# In practice, use External Secrets Operator to populate from AWS Secrets Manager
# — never commit actual secret values to this file.

# kubernetes/deployment.yaml (excerpt)
envFrom:
  - configMapRef:
      name: agent-config
env:
  - name: LLM_API_KEY
    valueFrom:
      secretKeyRef:
        name: agent-secrets
        key: LLM_API_KEY
  - name: DATABASE_URL
    valueFrom:
      secretKeyRef:
        name: agent-secrets
        key: DATABASE_URL
A self-contained script that reads config from the environment and builds an OpenAI client from those settings. Run it with the environment variables set in your shell or via a

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Minimal runnable example: environment-driven LLM call.

Set env vars before running:
  export LLM_API_KEY=sk-...
  export LLM_MODEL=gpt-4o-mini
  python3 minimal_agent.py
"""
import os
from openai import OpenAI
from pydantic_settings import BaseSettings, SettingsConfigDict


class Config(BaseSettings):
    model_config = SettingsConfigDict(env_file=".env", extra="ignore")
    llm_api_key: str           # required — raises if missing
    llm_model: str = "gpt-4o"
    llm_timeout: float = 30.0
    max_steps: int = 10
    log_level: str = "INFO"


def main() -> None:
    cfg = Config()
    print(f"[config] model={cfg.llm_model}, max_steps={cfg.max_steps}, "
          f"api_key=***{cfg.llm_api_key[-4:]}")

    client = OpenAI(api_key=cfg.llm_api_key, timeout=cfg.llm_timeout)

    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model=cfg.llm_model,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What is 2 + 2?"}],
        max_tokens=64,
    )
    print("[agent] response:", response.choices[0].message.content)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Example 2 — Multi-environment config with feature flag and tool gating

A production-grade settings module with nested sub-models, a feature-flag check, redacted logging, and a test showing how to override settings in a unit test without touching actual environment variables.

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Production-style settings with nested models, feature flags, and test overrides.
Install: pip install pydantic-settings pytest
"""
from __future__ import annotations
import logging
from typing import List, Literal
from pydantic import field_validator
from pydantic_settings import BaseSettings, SettingsConfigDict


# ── Settings ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
class ToolConfig(BaseSettings):
    model_config = SettingsConfigDict(env_prefix="TOOL_")
    enabled: List[str] = ["calculator"]
    max_calls_per_step: int = 3


class AgentConfig(BaseSettings):
    model_config = SettingsConfigDict(env_prefix="AGENT_", env_file=".env", extra="ignore")
    api_key: str                              # AGENT_API_KEY
    model: str = "gpt-4o"                    # AGENT_MODEL
    max_steps: int = 25
    environment: Literal["dev", "staging", "prod"] = "prod"
    enable_experimental_planner: bool = False  # AGENT_ENABLE_EXPERIMENTAL_PLANNER
    tools: ToolConfig = ToolConfig()

    @field_validator("max_steps")
    @classmethod
    def cap_steps(cls, v: int) -> int:
        return min(v, 100)

    def is_tool_enabled(self, tool_name: str) -> bool:
        return tool_name in self.tools.enabled

    def log_safe(self) -> dict:
        d = self.model_dump()
        d["api_key"] = f"sk-...{self.api_key[-4:]}"
        return d


# ── Application code that consumes config ─────────────────────────────────
def run_agent(cfg: AgentConfig) -> str:
    log = logging.getLogger(__name__)
    log.info("Effective config: %s", cfg.log_safe())

    if cfg.environment != "prod":
        log.warning("Running outside production — safety limits relaxed")

    if cfg.enable_experimental_planner:
        log.info("Experimental planner ENABLED")

    if not cfg.is_tool_enabled("web_search"):
        log.warning("web_search disabled in this environment")

    # Simulated agent loop
    return f"Agent ran with model={cfg.model}, steps={cfg.max_steps}"


# ── Tests (run with pytest or as __main__) ────────────────────────────────
def test_missing_api_key_raises() -> None:
    import pytest
    with pytest.raises(Exception):  # ValidationError
        AgentConfig(api_key="")  # empty string won't satisfy required str

def test_bool_flag_false_string() -> None:
    cfg = AgentConfig(api_key="sk-test1234", enable_experimental_planner=False)
    assert cfg.enable_experimental_planner is False

def test_tool_gating() -> None:
    cfg = AgentConfig(api_key="sk-test1234", tools=ToolConfig(enabled=["calculator"]))
    assert cfg.is_tool_enabled("calculator") is True
    assert cfg.is_tool_enabled("web_search") is False


if __name__ == "__main__":
    logging.basicConfig(level="INFO")
    # Simulated env (in real usage, these come from the process environment)
    import os
    os.environ.setdefault("AGENT_API_KEY", "sk-demo0000")
    os.environ.setdefault("AGENT_MODEL", "gpt-4o-mini")
    os.environ.setdefault("AGENT_MAX_STEPS", "15")
    os.environ.setdefault("TOOL_ENABLED", '["calculator","web_search"]')
    os.environ.setdefault("AGENT_ENABLE_EXPERIMENTAL_PLANNER", "false")

    cfg = AgentConfig()
    result = run_agent(cfg)
    print(result)

    # Run inline tests
    test_bool_flag_false_string()
    test_tool_gating()
    print("All inline tests passed.")

A customer-support agent deployed on AWS ECS with two task definitions — one for the API process and one for the background worker — shares most config via an SSM Parameter Store namespace but diverges on a few keys.

# SSM Parameter Store layout
/prod/agent/LLM_API_KEY          (SecureString) — fetched at container start
/prod/agent/DATABASE_URL         (SecureString)
/prod/agent/LLM_MODEL            (String) = gpt-4o
/prod/agent/AGENT_MAX_STEPS      (String) = 25
/prod/agent/LOG_LEVEL            (String) = INFO
/prod/agent/ENVIRONMENT          (String) = production
/prod/agent/ENABLE_HUMAN_HANDOFF (String) = true

# Worker-specific overrides (same namespace, different prefix)
/prod/worker/AGENT_MAX_STEPS     (String) = 50    # workers can run longer
/prod/worker/LOG_LEVEL           (String) = DEBUG  # more verbose
Each ECS task definition references these parameters using the
secret syntax. The ECS task execution role holds a least-privilege IAM policy granting
only on the specific ARN prefix — no role in the system holds write access to those parameters except the deployment pipeline.

When the LLM provider rotates the API key, the ops team updates the SSM SecureString and triggers an ECS service update that replaces running tasks with fresh ones that fetch the new key at startup. Zero code changes, zero image rebuilds. The rotation is complete in under two minutes with no request dropped, because ECS performs a rolling replacement and the old tasks continue serving until the new ones pass their health check.
The same pattern supports the staging environment: the staging task definitions point at
— reducing per-request cost by ~10× for equivalent functional testing.
Mistake 1 — Secrets in Dockerfile ENV or source code

# BAD: key is baked into every image layer, visible in docker history
ENV OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-real-key-here

# GOOD: no secret in the image; inject at runtime from secrets manager
ENV LLM_MODEL=gpt-4o
# OPENAI_API_KEY comes from the orchestrator's secret reference
Mistake 2 — Trusting bool() on raw env string

# BAD: bool("false") is True in Python — enables the destructive tool
enable_delete = bool(os.getenv("ENABLE_DELETE_TOOL", "false"))

# GOOD: let pydantic parse booleans; it knows "false" means False
class Cfg(BaseSettings):
    enable_delete_tool: bool = False  # env ENABLE_DELETE_TOOL=false → False ✓
Mistake 3 — Silent defaults that survive in production

# BAD: if AGENT_MODEL is never set in prod, the service silently uses the cheap model
class Settings(BaseSettings):
    agent_model: str = "gpt-4o-mini"  # looks safe, dangerous at scale

# BETTER: require the model to be explicit in non-dev environments
class Settings(BaseSettings):
    agent_model: str  # required — no default; task definition must set it
    environment: str = "production"
Mistake 4 — Logging raw settings at startup

# BAD: logs the API key to stdout / CloudWatch Logs / Datadog
log.info("Config: %s", settings.dict())

# GOOD: redact secrets before logging
log.info("Config: %s", settings.log_safe())

# .gitignore  — ensure this is present
.env
*.local.env
.env.*
!.env.example    # keep the example file — it documents every variable
— typed, validated settings with defaults and fail-fast behaviour. Never rely on naked
for anything that needs to be correct.
— the example documents every variable with its type, a safe non-secret default, and a one-line description. It acts as the contract between code and operations.
Inject secrets from a managed store
— AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, or Azure Key Vault, projected into the container at startup. Never embed in the image or task definition plaintext.
— the task execution role may read secrets; it must not write them. The developer role must not read production secrets.
Log effective config redacted at INFO on startup
before the service begins serving. This makes post-incident debugging trivial.
— the API process and background worker often need different
; maintain separate task definitions or config namespaces.
prefixes prevent collisions and make the source of each variable obvious in the task definition.
— keep ConfigMaps, SSM path values, and .env.example in an infrastructure repository with code review, just like application code.
Environment variable resolution is effectively free at runtime: the OS maintains the environment as a block of memory that any call to

reads in O(n) where n is the total size of the environment block (typically under 10 KB). The pydantic-settings validation run at import time adds a few milliseconds on startup — negligible compared with TCP socket setup or first LLM call latency, but worth knowing it is a one-time cost, not per-request.

At scale the interesting constraint is not variable access but configuration propagation. If 500 ECS tasks all need to pick up a new model name, a rolling restart touching all tasks takes minutes. For settings that must propagate faster than a rolling restart, move from env vars to a dynamic config service: each agent worker polls or subscribes to the flag service, and the new value takes effect in the current reasoning loop without a restart.

When using AWS Secrets Manager as the source of truth, be aware of the API rate limits. Fetching a secret at container startup is one call per container launch. At 1,000 container starts per minute (a large burst scaling event), that is 1,000 GetSecretValue calls/minute, which fits comfortably within the default quota. Avoid fetching secrets dynamically on every request — cache them in process memory after the first fetch and refresh on a background timer for rotation.

For serverless or short-lived container patterns (AWS Lambda, Cloud Run with min-instances=0), container cold starts mean every invocation may re-run the settings validation. pydantic-settings is fast enough that this is not a bottleneck in practice, but consider using a module-level singleton and caching the settings object (the standard Python module import cache handles this automatically as long as you instantiate Settings once at module level).

pip install pydantic-settings
# optional: extra sources
pip install pydantic-settings[dotenv]    # already included
pip install pydantic-settings[yaml]      # YAML source support

# Reading from multiple sources — env takes priority over .env file
from pydantic_settings import BaseSettings, SettingsConfigDict, YamlSettingsSource

class MultiSourceSettings(BaseSettings):
    model_config = SettingsConfigDict(
        env_file=".env",
        yaml_file="config.yaml",   # lowest priority
    )
    model: str = "gpt-4o"
    max_steps: int = 25

# ExternalSecret syncs AWS Secrets Manager → Kubernetes Secret automatically
apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1beta1
kind: ExternalSecret
metadata:
  name: agent-secrets
spec:
  refreshInterval: 1h
  secretStoreRef:
    name: aws-secrets-manager
    kind: SecretStore
  target:
    name: agent-secrets
    creationPolicy: Owner
  data:
    - secretKey: LLM_API_KEY
      remoteRef:
        key: /prod/agent/LLM_API_KEY
    - secretKey: DATABASE_URL
      remoteRef:
        key: /prod/agent/DATABASE_URL
These SaaS secrets platforms provide a developer-friendly UI for managing secrets across environments and integrate with Docker Compose, Kubernetes, and CI/CD pipelines through a CLI injector:
populates the process environment from the configured secrets project without a single secret ever touching a local file or CI variable storage.
Nested config, tool permission lists, prompt bundles
No — use Secret for sensitive values
AWS Secrets Manager / Vault (direct API fetch)
Secrets requiring fine-grained RBAC, audit trail
Flags that must flip without restart, % rollouts

In practice the recommended approach is a combination: environment variables for secrets and flat settings (via secrets manager injection), a mounted YAML for complex nested config, and a flag service only when live-flip capability is a genuine requirement. Avoid reaching for complexity early.
, hardcoded strings, default values that happen to be real keys)
in a shell script that appears in CI logs)
Application logs — always redact before logging
Environment variable dumps in error pages, debug endpoints, or health-check responses
The ECS task execution role (or Kubernetes service account) should hold the minimum permissions to retrieve secrets at startup. Use a dedicated IAM policy that grants

only on the specific parameter ARN prefix for that service, not a wildcard. When a secret is rotated, the updated value should propagate automatically: External Secrets Operator polls Secrets Manager on a configurable interval and updates the Kubernetes Secret, triggering a rolling pod restart.
A service that starts with missing config is a ticking time bomb: it may serve traffic for minutes before the first request hits the unconfigured code path. Fail fast by placing the

call at module level. The process will exit before the HTTP listener starts if any required variable is absent. Combine this with an orchestrator health check that the load balancer can use to decide whether to route traffic — if the process never starts, health checks fail, and the old tasks continue serving until the issue is resolved.

When the same config is shared across multiple containers (API, worker, scheduler), centralise it in one ConfigMap or SSM namespace rather than duplicating values in each task definition. Duplication leads to partial updates where the API is running gpt-4o but the worker is still on gpt-4o-mini, producing inconsistent behaviour that is very hard to diagnose.

Environment variables are the standard mechanism for supplying runtime config to containers; use them for secrets, per-environment settings, and feature flags — never for deeply nested or frequently-changing config.
Always use a typed settings library (pydantic-settings for Python, envalid for TypeScript); naked
misses type coercion, bool parsing, and validation, which causes silent production bugs.
files committed to git; inject them at container start from AWS Secrets Manager, Vault, or a Kubernetes Secret.
Fail fast: instantiate the settings object at module import time so the process exits with a clear error before accepting any traffic if a required variable is absent or has the wrong type.
Log a redacted snapshot of the effective config at INFO level on startup; this single practice eliminates the majority of post-incident config-mismatch debugging.
Use least-privilege IAM: the container's runtime role reads secrets; no other identity writes them; developers cannot read production secrets.

When values must change without a container restart (real-time flag flips, sub-minute propagation), use a dynamic feature-flag service rather than environment variables, which require a rolling restart to take effect.
committed to the repository is the contract between code and operations — it must document every variable, its type, a safe default, and a one-line description.
